coredata.dc:title: Highly crystalline poly-3-hexylthiophene particles prepared from Pickering emulsions stabilized by alkylamine functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yunmi
Im, Sang Hyuk
coredata.dc:description: Pickering emulsions have received much attention due to their high emulsions stability and ability to form functional emulsions compared to conventional emulsions. The stability of Pickering emulsions is dependent on the geometry and wettability of the particle type emulsifiers, which affect the detachment energy at the interface. Therefore, surface property control of the particle is essential. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with octylamine (C8), dodecylamine (C12), and hexadecylamine (C16) were synthesized and the stability of oil-in-water type Pickering emulsions compared. The detachment energies of the Pickering emulsifiers were calculated by measuring their wettability. Crystalline poly-3-hexylthiopene nanoparticles (P3HT NPs) were synthesized from the Pickering emulsion with C12-GQDs. Among the three GQDs, the C12-GQDs formed the most stable Pickering emulsions at the water-CHCl3 interface because of an appropriate balance between the water and oil phases due to the highest detachment energy. High-crystalline P3HT NPs can be synthesized from the Pickering emulsion with C12-GQDs due to the slower solvent evaporation and enhanced stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Amphiphilicity
Pickering emulsion
Solvent barrier properties
P3HT nanoparticle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs
Formation of Pickering emulsions by C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs, and emulsions by SDS
Synthesis of P3HT NPs with Pickering emulsions by C12-GQDs
Synthesis and characterization of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs
Characterization of the Pickering emulsions stabilized by GQDs
Preparation of P3HT particles from the Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), grants funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (Grant No. 2022R1A2C3004964)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) TEM images of (a) C8-GQDs, (b) C12-GQDs, and (c) C16-GQDs: upper inset = histogram for size distribution of the GQDs, lower inset = HR-TEM image; and (d-f) UV–vis absorbance spectra (black line) and photoluminescence spectra (gradation-colored solid lines) of the (d) C8-GQDs, (e) C12-GQDs, and (f) C16-GQDs solution: legends of all graphs = excitation wavelength.

(a-c) Optical microscopic images of CHCl3-in-water Pickering emulsions stabilized by (a) C8-GQDs, (b) C12-GQDs, and (c) C16-GQDs: upper inset = photograph, lower inset = histogram for size distribution of the Pickering emulsions; (d) Sessile drop method 3-phase contact angles of substrates coated by C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs, and C16-GQDs, and (e) graphs of the calculated detachment energies of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs: doted lines = theoretical values, big solid dots = experimental values.

(a-d) Optical microscopic images of CHCl3-in-water Pickering emulsions stabilized by (a) 1.05 mg/mL, (b) 2.1 mg/mL, (c) 3.15 mg/mL, and (d) 4.2 mg/mL concentration of C12-GQDs and (e) 8.38 mM (1CMC) concentration of SDS, and (f) dynamic surface tensions with pendant drop method of corresponding C12-GQDs and SDS solution.

(a) Schematic illustration of the procedure for the preparation of P3HT NPs using C12-GQDs as emulsifier, The sonication temperature was maintained at 25 ℃ and sonication was performed for 10 min. The emulsion of P3HT was evaporated in CHCl3 at 70 ℃ for 3 h, (b-e) SEM images of P3HT NPs prepared by (b) 1.05 mg/mL, (c) 2.10 mg/mL, (d) 3.15 mg/mL and (e) 4.20 mg/mL concentration of C12-GQDs (inset = histogram for the size distribution of the P3HT NPs), and (f) TEM image of P3HT NPs prepared by 3.15 mg/mL concentration of C12-GQDs.

(a) Photographs of P3HT/CHCl3 solution and P3HT NPs emulsions prepared with SDS, a molecular surfactant (P3HT@SDS) and C12-GQD, a particulate surfactant (P3HT@C12-GQDs) after sonication and schematic illustration of corresponding interface and (b-d) thermal and optical properties of P3HT NPs with SDS, a molecular surfactant and C12-GQD, a particulate surfactant: (b) DSC curve measured at 10℃/min, (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra, and (d) photoluminescence spectra (inset = TRPL spectra).

head.ce:title: Highly crystalline poly-3-hexylthiophene particles prepared from Pickering emulsions stabilized by alkylamine functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BK21 Four R&E Center, Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University, Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BK21 Four R&E Center, Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BK21 Four R&E Center, Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pickering emulsions have received much attention due to their high emulsions stability and ability to form functional emulsions compared to conventional emulsions. The stability of Pickering emulsions is dependent on the geometry and wettability of the particle type emulsifiers, which affect the detachment energy at the interface. Therefore, surface property control of the particle is essential. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with octylamine (C8), dodecylamine (C12), and hexadecylamine (C16) were synthesized and the stability of oil-in-water type Pickering emulsions compared. The detachment energies of the Pickering emulsifiers were calculated by measuring their wettability. Crystalline poly-3-hexylthiopene nanoparticles (P3HT NPs) were synthesized from the Pickering emulsion with C12-GQDs. Among the three GQDs, the C12-GQDs formed the most stable Pickering emulsions at the water-CHCl3 interface because of an appropriate balance between the water and oil phases due to the highest detachment energy. High-crystalline P3HT NPs can be synthesized from the Pickering emulsion with C12-GQDs due to the slower solvent evaporation and enhanced stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pickering emulsions have a stabilized interface where solid particle-type surfactants are irreversibly assembled at the interface of immiscible liquids, so they are more stable than conventional emulsions formed with molecular-type surfactants where coalescence and Ostwald ripening occur through repeated adsorption and desorption [1]. In addition, by forming an emulsion with an interface with unique properties of solid particles, emulsions with multifunctional properties can be manufactured, and thus have received great attention in various application fields such as catalysts and drug delivery [2,3].

The wettability of the particle determines the type of Pickering emulsion [4] because the hydrophilic (or hydrophobic) particles with the contact angle of smaller than (or larger than) 90° stabilize the oil-in-water (or water-in-oil) emulsion, respectively. In contrast, when the contact angle of the particle is close to 90°, it is adsorbed with a balanced force to the oil and water phases, which has the maximum fixation energy that the particle can have among all contact angles [5]. Therefore, balancing the wettability of the particles to the two immiscible phases by varying the surface properties of the solid particles is very important to create stable Pickering emulsions.

Another factor that affects the stability of Pickering emulsion is the shape of the particle because the area that can occupy the interface varies depending on the shape of the particle. Recently, research on surfactants with a two-dimensional (2D) plate structure is increasing because the 2D plates can be arranged in parallel at the liquid-liquid interface, so the ratio of elements that can participate in stabilization is higher than that of spherical and cubic surfactants, resulting in higher efficiency compared to the same mass [6–14]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a representative 2D plate structure with amphiphilic properties due to its hydrophobic sp2 structure and hydrophilic oxygen functional group as a surfactant [15,16]. The sp2 structure of GO induces multi-tiling through the π-π stacking effect, increases interfacial coverage, and can form stable emulsions [17]. Despite these advantages, GO, which has a size of several tens of microns, has the disadvantage of low interfacial activity because it can only produce millimeter-sized emulsions and agglomeration easily occurs [18,19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nm-sized carbon materials and are composed of sp2 carbon group and oxygen functional group. Like the behavior of GO at the interface, GQDs can be easily formed into well-controlled nanostructures through bottom-up synthesis methods. The approach allows selective incorporation of heteroatom doping and various functional groups, and control of electron density, particle size, and wettability properties. Hence, GQDs have received much attention in the fields of catalysts, optoelectronics, and biotechnologies [20–22]. Unlike amphiphilic GO, GQDs, which are hydrophilic due to a higher proportion of oxygen groups compared to the graphite core, have low surface activity. Therefore, it is an important issue to design GQDs with appropriate wettability through structural modification [23–25]. To solve this problem, designs that control the wettability of GQDs have been reported. Yang et al. investigated the activity of GQDs as a surfactant through reduction of the oxygen functional group [26]. Cho et al. confirmed the ratio of optimal surfactant ability depending on the ratio of hexylamine bound to the surface of GQDs [27]. Wang et al. confirmed that they had higher surface activity than existing surfactants by functionalizing GQDs with dodecylamine [28]. Surface-treated GQDs have been confirmed to be effective materials for stabilizing the interface, but few cases have been reported that theoretically prove the stability of the interface occupied by the particles under each condition and confirm the synergy effect between particles and emulsions.

In the present study, GQDs functionalized with octylamine (C8), dodecylamine (C12), and hexadecylamine (C16) were synthesized through a bottom-up process to prepare particle-type surfactants with appropriate wettability in oil and water that can form stable Pickering emulsions. By investigating the particle size and wettability characteristics that vary depending on the length of the alkyl chain and calculating the detachment energy when Pickering emulsions were created, the GQDs that can produce the most stable Pickering emulsions were identified. In addition, poly-3-hexylthiophene nanoparticles (P3HT NPs), a model conjugated polymer particle, were created through the formation of stable Pickering emulsions using GQDs, a particle-type surfactant. P3HT NPs synthesized through Pickering emulsions were found to have different properties from P3HT NPs prepared using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), a molecular surfactant.

1-Hexanol (Junsei), D-(+)-glucose (Sigma-Aldrich), acetic acid (glacial, Sigma-Aldrich), octylamine (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), dodecylamine (98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and hexadecylamine (90 %, Sigma-Aldrich), methanol (Merk), ethanol (anhydrous, 99.9 %, Samchun), chloroform (99.5 %, Samchun), 3.5 kDa dialysis bag (Spectra/Por 3, SpectrumTM), and P3HT (LT-S909, Lumtec) were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs were synthesized by following our previous report [29].

Briefly, 9 mL of hexanol was bubbled with nitrogen gas in a 25 mL 3-neck round bottom flask (RBF) for 1 h. Afterwards, RBF was immersed in an oil bath at 80 °C, 1 mL of 10 wt% glucose solution and 0.2 mL of acetic acid were injected, and stirred in a closed system at 1500 rpm for 1 h. 0.6141 g of dodecylamine dissolved in 1 mL of hexanol was injected into RBF, the oil bath was heated to 150 °C, and reaction was performed for 1 h under nitrogen purge conditions. After the reaction was completed, hexanol was removed through rotary evaporation, the solvent was changed to methanol, and large particles were removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. Afterwards, dialysis (3.5 kDa) purification was performed with the sample dispersed in ethanol for 7 days. The samples were refrigerated until use. C8-GQDs and C16-GQDs were made by adding 0.4282 g of octylamine and 0.8 g of hexadecylamine instead of dodecylamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of Pickering emulsions by C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs, and emulsions by SDS
ce:section.ce:para: First, C8-GQD, C12-GQD, and C16-GQD were dispersed in water at a concentration of 3.15 mg/mL and then sonicated for 5 min. To form Pickering emulsions, aqueous GQDs:CHCl3 were prepared in respective ratios and emulsified using a vortex mixer for 3 min. 8.38 mM of SDS (1 CMC) was stirred to dissolve in water. In the same way as the Pickering emulsion, SDS solution:CHCl3 was prepared according to each ratio and emulsified using a vortex mixer for 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of P3HT NPs with Pickering emulsions by C12-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To form P3HT emulsions, C12-GQDs of each concentration were dispersed in water and put through ultrasonication for 5 min. 0.1 mL of P3HT (10 mg/mL) dissolved in CHCl3 was added to 1 mL of aqueous GQDs solution. After adding the P3HT solution to the vial, the mixture was sonicated for 10 min using a bath sonicator to create emulsions. The CHCl3 inside the emulsions was evaporated while stirring in an oil bath at 70 ℃ for 3 h.

The morphology of the GQDs was obtained with a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai 20, FEI) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The bonding strength was obtained using a 630 FT-IR spectrometer (Agilent). The optical properties of the GQDs were obtained with a Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrum using a UV–VIS–NIR spectrometer (UV-3600Plus, Shimadzu) and Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum using a fluorophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu). Contact angle was measured with the Phoenix-MT series (Surface electro Optics). The crystallinity of P3HT NPs was confirmed by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) using Perkin Elmer equipment (DSC4000, 10 °C/min). The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured using a photon-counting spectrofluorometer (PC1, ISS) under the irradiation of a 373 nm picosecond laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize GQDs with an alkylamine functional group, Amadori products were generated through a nucleophilic substitution reaction between glucose and alkylamine such as C8, C12, and C16, and single crystal GQDs with C8 (C8-GQDs), C12 (C12-GQDs), and C16 (C16-GQDs) functional group were synthesized through a spontaneous dehydration reaction between these products as shown in Fig. 1 (a)-(c) [29]. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) analysis was performed to confirm whether the designed structure was formed as shown in Fig. S1. The peak centered at 2845–2952 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational transition of CH2/CH3, and the intensity was increased proportionally with the length of the alkyl chains bonded on the surface [30]. Additionally, peaks corresponding to C-N, C-O, N-H, and CC bonding were confirmed at 1364 cm−1, 1460 cm−1, 1555 cm−1, and 1650 cm−1 [20,31,32]. The peak at 1650 cm−1, indicating sp2 carbon structure, tended to increase as the bonded alkyl chain becomes shorter, which is presumed to be due to an increase in particle size. As shown in Fig. S2, XPS confirmed the binding of the functional groups of the three GQDs. The C-C/CC bands were confirmed at 284.7–284.8 eV in the C1s peak, indicating the presence of sp2 carbon bonding, consistent with the FT-IR analysis previously analyzed. In the N1s peak, bonding corresponding to C-N and N-H was confirmed at 399.5–399.7 eV and 400.5–401.5 eV for all three GQDs, which means that the alkyl amine is successfully bonded to the GQDs [33–35]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to check the size and d-spacing of GQDs. The average lateral size of the particles was observed to be ∼3.8 nm for C8-GQDs, ∼3.4 nm for C12-GQDs, and ∼3.0 nm for C16-GQDs, respectively, as shown in inset histograms of Fig. 1 (a)-(c). The inset HR-TEM (high resolution-TEM) images show a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm, corresponding to the (100) hexagonal lattice spacing along the [001] direction present in the graphene structure [36]. An increase in the alkylamine chain length of the functional group resulted in a decrease in particle size. These results might be attributed to the fact that the production rate of the Amadori products, which are formed by the nucleophilic substitution reaction of glucose with alkylamines, increases with increasing chain length of the alkylamine because the nucleophilicity of the alkylamine is proportional to its chain length [37].

Absorption and emission spectra of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs and C16-GQDs were shown in Fig. 1 (d)-(f). All samples were measured in a solution state of GQDs/ethanol and the concentrations of GQDs/ethanol solutions were fixed to an absorbance of 1 at 230 nm. The UV–visible spectra of the three GQDs/ethanol solutions showed strong absorption at 230 nm due to CC (π–π*) and CO and C-N (n−π*, 280 nm, 350 nm) peak intensities were gradually weakened as the particle size decreased [38]. Excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission, known as a unique characteristic of GQDs, was commonly observed in the three samples, and this result was attributed to energy states generated by the surface functional groups of GQDs [39]. In Fig. S3, the maximum emission wavelengths of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs, and C16-GQDs were blue-shifted to ∼476 nm, ∼466 nm, and ∼464 nm as the functionalized carbon chain length became longer. It is well consistent with the previous report that the emission wavelength red-shifts as the particle grows[24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the Pickering emulsions stabilized by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the Pickering emulsions having alkylamine functionalized GQDs-surfactant was checked as shown in Fig. 2 (a)-(c), of which the Pickering emulsions with oil-in-water system were formed with CHCl3 (oil phase) and GQDs solution (water phase) in a volume ratio of 1:3. The optical microscopic images clearly indicate that the C12-GQDs sample is the most stable Pickering emulsion among the three samples. The average sizes of the emulsion droplets in optical microscopic images were ∼24.3 μm (C8-GQDs), ∼1.9 μm (C12-GQDs), and ∼3.3 μm (C16-GQDs), respectively. The formation of the smallest stable emulsion droplets by C12-GQDs implies that the C12-GQDs Pickering-surfactant made the most stabilized interface in the present condition. The wettability of GQD-particles, which is one of the factors determining the type and stability of Pickering emulsions, was measured through the 3-phase contact angle as shown in Fig. 2 (d). The contact angles indicate that the C8-GQDs and the C16-GQDs have hydrophilic and hydrophobic characteristics because their contact angles were 60º and 113.5º. Due to the hydrophilicity of the C8-GQDs, the less stable emulsion drops with large size were formed in the oil-in-water system. The C16-GQDs formed the non-spherical and wrinkled emulsion droplets because they have strong aggregation in water due to the high hydrophobicity of C16-GQDs and the increased detachment energy between droplets[40]. In contrast, the C12-GQDs had a contact angle of 84.9º, close to 90º and consequently had a balanced wettability between hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties. Accordingly, the C12-GQDs sample showed high emulsions stability at the interface due to the high surface activity of C12-GQDs.

The stability of the Pickering emulsions was greatly dependent on the characteristics of GQD-particles, as confirmed by optical microscopy, and it can be explained by the detachment energy of the GQD-particles at the interface between the two phases. The detachment energy (∆Gd) at the oil-water interface in Pickering emulsions is notably influenced by geometric factors such as surface wettability and the size and shape of particles [41]. As can be seen from Young’s equation, the 3-phase contact angle is determined by the interfacial tension between particles-water, particles-oil, and water-oil.
(1) cosθ = (γparticle-oil - γparticle-water) / γoil-particle
where γparticle-oil is the interfacial tensions (surface free energies) of the particle-oil interface, γparticle-water is the interfacial tensions (surface free energies) of the particle-water interface and γoil-water is the interfacial tensions (surface free energies) of the oil-water interface.

The detachment energy of particles at the oil-water interface is as follows [4].
(2) ∆Gdw = γoil-water (Ac - Apo cosθ)
(3) ∆Gdo = γoil-water (Ac + Apw cosθ)
where Ac is the area of the oil–water interface occupied by the particle when it is occupied at the fluid interface, Apo and Apw are the area of the particle-oil or water interfaces in equilibrium state and θ represents the contact angle created by solid particles at the oil-water interface. GQDs are 2D structured particles with a thickness of 1–3 layers and a particle size of less than 20 nm. Therefore, in the equilibrium state, the GQDs are located parallel to the water-oil interface, so the areas in contact with each phase (oil and water) are the same. (Ac, Apo, and Apw are same) Hence, the detachment energy of oil and water system can be expressed as follows.
(4) ∆Gdw = γoil-water πr2 (1 - cosθ) (0° ≤ θ ≤ 90°)
(5) ∆Gdo = γoil-water πr2 (1 + cosθ) (90° ≤ θ ≤ 180°)

The above equations can be finally expressed as follows[6].
(6) ∆Gd = γoil-water πr2 (1 - |cosθ|)

Using the above equation, the detachment energy at the interface was calculated for three GQDs with their sizes and contact angles and plotted in Fig. 2 (e). The calculated detachment energies of C8-GQDs, C12-GQDs, and C16-GQDs were 8.8×103 kBT, 1.3×104 kBT, and 6.8×103 kBT, respectively. Therefore, the C12-GQDs, which maintain an appropriate balance between the water and oil phases, have the highest detachment energy and form the most stable Pickering emulsions at the water-CHCl3 interface among the three GQDs.

The change in Pickering emulsion stability according to pH is shown in Fig. S4. When the pH was 7, the emulsion stabilized with C12-GQDs remained the most stable. This is because when the pH is low, the particles aggregate due to protonation, and when the pH is high, repulsion between particles occurs due to deprotonation [42,43]. The effect of C12-GQDs concentration on the size and stability of Pickering emulsions were checked as shown in Fig. 3. The concentrations of C12-GQDs were varied from 1.05 mg/mL, 2.1 mg/mL, 3.15 mg/mL, and 4.2 mg/mL. As the concentration of C12-GQDs increased from 1.05 mg/mL to 3.15 mg/mL, the average droplet size of the emulsions decreased to ∼2.46 μm, ∼1.38 μm, and ∼1.33 μm, respectively. As the concentration of GQD-particles increases, more particles occupy the interface and causes smaller and more stable emulsion droplets. Even after a week, all Pickering emulsions were stable without coalescence and maintained their initial droplet-sizes as shown in Fig. S5. Additionally, even after three weeks of storage, emulsions stabilized with GQDs were maintained without phase separation as shown in Fig. S6. On the other hand, emulsions manufactured with SDS went through unstable processes such as Ostwald ripening or coalescence over time, increasing the size of the emulsions and decreasing the density of emulsions. This indicates that the Pickering emulsions stabilized by C12-GQDs have better long-term storage stability than the emulsion manufactured with SDS. On the other hand, when further increasing the concentration of C12-GQDs to 4.2 mg/mL, the droplet size slightly increased to ∼1.48 μm. To further understand the effect of C12-GQDs concentration on the emulsions stability, the variations of dynamic surface tensions at the air/water interface were measured as shown in Fig. 3 (f). With the increase in the concentration of C12-GQDs, the surface tension decreased to ∼27.6, ∼23.6, and ∼23.2 mN/m, respectively, but the surface tension of 4.2 mg/mL C12-GQDs increased to ∼31.42 mN/m. The increase in surface tension at high concentrations of C12-GQDs means that the surface activity decreases at high concentrations of GQDs, which is consistent with the increased size of emulsion droplet. The low surface activity is attributed to that GQDs with sp2 structures increase the particle size through π- π interaction, which leads to a decrease in the detachment energy at the interface [44,45]. To compare the emulsions stability of C12-GQDs samples, emulsions droplet size and surface tension were measured using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), a conventional molecular surfactant. The average droplet size of the emulsions formed by SDS was ∼12.82 μm, as shown in Fig. 3 (e), which is larger than that of the emulsions formed by C12-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (f), the surface tension of SDS is ∼36.67 mN/m, showing lower surface activity than C12-GQDs. This indicates that the C12-GQDs particle type surfactants have better surface activity than the SDS molecular type surfactant and are more suitable to form stable emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P3HT particles from the Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To find application of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-GQDs, we tried to prepare photo-active poly-3-hexylthiophene nanoparticles (P3HT NPs) as a model conjugated polymer NP. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), the P3HT Pickering emulsion was prepared by adding 0.1 mL P3HT/CHCl3 solution (10 mg/mL) in 1 mL C12-GQDs/water solution and subsequent sonication. The Pickering emulsions were then heat treated under magnetically stirring to remove the CHCl3 and the P3HT NPs were obtained. At this time, Pickering emulsions maintained their emulsion shape even during heat treatment as shown in Fig. S7. Fig. 4 (b)-(e) showed the SEM images of the prepared P3HT NPs and the particle size distribution. The sizes of P3HT NPs with the concentration of C12-GQDs had similar tendency with the droplet-size of CHCl3/water Pickering emulsion. As the concentration of C12-GQDs increased, the average sizes of P3HT NPs gradually decreased to ∼164.48 nm for 1.05 mg/mL sample, ∼160.47 nm for 2.10 mg/mL, and ∼138.66 nm for 3.15 mg/mL while forming more uniform size distribution. However, the 4.20 mg/mL of C12-CQDs had ∼269.59 nm of average size of P3HT NPs and broader size distribution. The formation of larger P3HT NPs is attributed to the lowered interfacial detachment energy, which causes coalescence of emulsion droplets during the heat treatment process under magnetically stirring [46]. Fig. S8 shows the size of particles dispersed in water was confirmed through Dynamic light scattering (DLS). The average size of P3HT NPs was ∼214.8 nm for 1.05 mg/mL sample, ∼212.7 nm for 2.10 mg/mL, 176 nm for 3.15 mg/mL and ∼349 nm for 4.20 mg/mL. Because DLS measures the hydrodynamic diameter of particles [47], the size was measured to be larger than that of the particles measured by SEM, but the particle diameter tendency according to the concentration measured in the SEM image was consistent. TEM image in Fig. 4 (f) indicates that the C12-GQDs adsorbed on the surface of P3HT NPs and effectively formed stable P3HT NPs.

In addition, the particle type C12-GQDs with sp2 carbon structure are expected to act as a solvent barrier and retard the solvent evaporation from the dispersed phase of the formed emulsions, leading to a change in the crystallinity of P3HT NPs. Fig. 5 (a) is photographs of P3HT/CHCl3 solution and P3HT NPs emulsions prepared with SDS a molecular surfactant and C12-GQD a particulate surfactant after sonication. The P3HT NPs emulsion with SDS surfactant revealed dark brown in color due to the evaporation of CHCl3 during sonication, whereas the P3HT NPs emulsion with C12-GQD surfactant cloudy orange in color, which is close color of the P3HT/CHCl3 solution, due to the presence of CHCl3 [48].

To confirm if the SDS molecular surfactant and the GQDs particulate surfactant affect the crystallization of P3HT NPs, differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) and absorption spectra were measured [49,50]. Fig. 5 (b) is the heating DSC curves of SDS, P3HT@SDS NPs, and P3HT@C12-GQD NPs. The P3HT@C12-GQD NPs had a melting point (Tm) at 251 °C, but the P3HT@SDS NPs did not have Tm peak [51]. The heating DSC curves clearly indicate that the P3HT@C12-GQD NPs have high crystallinity and the P3HT@SDS NPs do not show detectable Tm peak, which comes from crystalline region, due to quick evaporation of CHCl3 [52]. Absorption spectra of the P3HT@SDS NPs and P3HT@C12-GQD NPs were shown in Fig. 5 (c). The three absorption peaks appearing in the 400–750 nm region are known to be caused by disordered P3HT chains (∼513 nm) and aggregated P3HT chains (∼556 nm and ∼608 nm). Additionally, the red-shift in the 0–1, 0–2 vibration band of the absorption spectra indicates an increase in the average conjugation length of P3HT aggregates and an increase in the aggregated structure [53,54]. Spano et al. confirmed the correlation between the change in intensity ratio of A0–0 and A0–1 and the order within the chain [55]. The shape and size of the absorption peak of the synthesized P3HT NPs varied depending on the materials used to stabilize the P3HT emulsions. P3HT@C12-GQDs NPs were confirmed to have a red-shift tendency compared to P3HT@SDS NPs, and the A0–0/A0–1 ratio increased from 0.6 to 0.76, indicating that when emulsions were formed with C12-GQDs, interchain interaction was relaxed, intrachain interaction was preferred, and a more ordered P3HT chain was formed. P3HT NPs synthesized by forming emulsions with C12-GQDs favored intrachain interaction as interchain interaction became looser, meaning that a more ordered P3HT chain was formed. Previous studies have reported that when forming a crystalline P3HT film, high bp solvents are used to provide time for crystals to grow, thereby forming a highly crystalline P3HT film [49,50]. Accordingly, the C12-GQDs with solvent evaporation properties reduced the solvent evaporation rate inside the P3HT emulsions, providing crystal growth time, which positively influenced the high crystal growth of the P3HT chain.

Additional information about the aggregation and interaction of P3HT NPs was obtained through steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence (PL and TRPL) analysis as shown in Fig. 5 (d). Emission peaks due to 0–0 and 0–1 transitions were confirmed in the PL of P3HT@SDS NPs and P3HT@C12-GQDs NPs. In the PL spectrum of conjugated polymer, the 0–0 peak is related to the recombination of exciton in the intra-chain state, and 0–1 is the emission peak that appears in the inter-chain state [56]. P3HT@C12-GQDs NPs had stronger PL emission and 0–0 peak ratio compared to P3HT@SDS NPs as shown in Fig. S9. These results suggest that the chain of P3HT NPs made of C12-GQDs has a higher level of planarity by weakening interactions between interchains and favoring interactions between intrachains. It was confirmed that P3HT NPs made with C12-GQDs have higher crystallinity than P3HT NPs made with SDS, and that interchain interactions are weakened and intrachain interactions are strengthened. As the interchain interaction between P3HT formed with C12-GQDs was weakened, the planarity of the P3HT chain backbone increased and polaron formation decreased, which modified the electronic and optical properties [57]. In other words, the increase in PL emission intensity of P3HT stabilized with C12-GQDs was due to its structure in which intrachain interaction is dominant [58]. Inset of Fig. 5 (d), the PL decay curves of the two samples are shown, calculated using two exponential components for proper fitting [59]. The average lifetime of P3HT@SDS NPs and P3HT@C12-GQDs NPs was 0.92 ns and 0.55 ns. The decrease in lifetime is the result of an increase in the intrachain interaction rate within the P3HT chain, and consistent with the previous UV and PL data, it can be seen that this phenomenon occurs in J type aggregates [60].

In summary, the GQDs functionalized by C8-amine, C12-amine and C16-amine were systematically investigated as a Pickering emulsifier. The size and wettability of GQDs was dependent on the alkyl amines bound on the GQDs and affected the interfacial stability of water and CHCl3. Among them, C12-GQDs formed the most stable oil-in-water emulsions due to balanced wettability toward oil and water and showed superior surface activity than the other GQDs- surfactants. The Pickering emulsions with functionalized by C12-GQDs were used to prepare crystalline photoactive P3HT NPs. Due to the retarded evaporation of solvent and excellent emulsion stability by C12-GQDs-surfactant, the produced P3HT NPs had better crystallinity and smaller particle size than the P3HT NPs prepared by conventional SDS molecular type surfactant. We believe that the stable Pickering emulsion with functionalized GQDs can be applicable to synthesize new class of particles with controlled crystallinity and morphology.

Sang Hyuk Im: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yunmi Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Sang Hyuk Im reports a relationship with National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) that includes: funding grants. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), grants funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (Grant No. 2022R1A2C3004964)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134819.
